summary: Nadia explores the idea of organized voter abstention as a legitimate form of political protest, pushing back against the reflexive moral outrage that greets any suggestion of not voting. She recounts proposing a mass abstention campaign to politically-minded peers and being met with derision. Drawing on Hirschman's exit/voice framework, she argues that abstention represents a valid 'exit' response to deteriorating political choices. She contends that society's infatuation with voting as the floor of civic engagement prevents honest examination of the voting system's structural flaws. While not personally advocating against voting, she calls for not-voting to be accepted as a legitimate tool of peaceful protest alongside other radical democratic proposals.
key_points: Organized voter abstention can be a deliberate political stance signifying dissatisfaction with the system, not merely laziness or apathy
Using Hirschman's exit/voice framework, abstention represents a valid 'exit' option that society refuses to acknowledge as legitimate civic engagement
The universal push to encourage voting, while appearing nonpartisan, actually discourages critical examination of the voting system itself
Not-voting provokes disproportionate moral outrage compared to equally radical proposals like democratic lotteries or liquid democracy
Treating voting as the minimum bar for civic engagement sets an artificial threshold that forecloses deeper discussion of political infrastructure
money_quotes: Citizens could stage a walkout from elections, like going on strike but from their own country.
Characterizing abstention as apathy or laziness, rather than a form of civic engagement that rightfully probes at our ability to 'exit,' seems uncharitable.
I worry that our infatuation with voting sets the bar for civic engagement at an artificial place, which makes it difficult to discuss our political systems more comprehensively.
While asking people to vote might be nonpartisan on a per-election level, it discourages us from critically examining the system of voting itself, which has known problems, especially at scale.
If not during an election cycle, while voting is top of mind for everyone, when is the right time to discuss these flaws?
The idea of not voting seems to provoke a sort of disgust and moral outrage that, say, democratic lotteries or liquid democracy, does not, even though these are arguably equally radical proposals.
Without leaving the problem space open for honest inquiry and exploration, it's hard to imagine how our political systems—not just the players, but its underlying infrastructure—will evolve.
key_insight: Society's reflexive moral outrage at voter abstention forecloses honest examination of the voting system's structural flaws, making it harder to evolve our political infrastructure beyond its current form.
